items.subtopic: Health Effects of Climate Change | EFFECTS OF CLIMATE CHANGE ON HEALTH
items.title: Impact of Climate Change on Global Health Mortality
items.content: Climate change is predicted to significantly increase mortality rates due to undernutrition, malaria, diarrhea, and heat stress. The World Health Organization estimates an additional 250,000 deaths between 2030 and 2050 attributable to these factors.
keyPoints: Additional 250,000 deaths projected from 2030 to 2050
Causes include undernutrition, malaria, diarrhea, and heat stress
Disproportionate impact on low- and middle-income countries (LMICs)
Weakened health infrastructures in LMICs due to neocolonialism and structural adjustment
items.reference: Health Effects of Climate Change · Health Effects of Climate Change | EFFECTS OF CLIMATE CHANGE ON HEALTH

items.subtopic: Health Effects of Climate Change | EFFECTS OF CLIMATE CHANGE ON HEALTH
items.title: Climate Change Exacerbating Health Disparities in Wealthier Nations
items.content: In wealthier countries like the United States, climate change is expected to worsen existing health disparities among racial groups. Populations such as black, Indigenous, and Latinx communities are likely to be more adversely affected.
keyPoints: Climate change amplifies health disparities in wealthy nations
Black, Indigenous, and Latinx populations disproportionately impacted
items.reference: Health Effects of Climate Change · Health Effects of Climate Change | EFFECTS OF CLIMATE CHANGE ON HEALTH

items.subtopic: AIR POLLUTION AND SYNERGISTIC EFFECTS
items.title: Impact of Air Pollution on Respiratory and Cardiovascular Diseases
items.content: Air pollution, particularly fine particulate matter (PM2.5), exacerbates respiratory ailments like asthma, COPD, and lung cancer, as well as cardiovascular diseases such as myocardial infarction and heart failure. Climate change increases ground-level ozone and PM2.5 concentrations, worsening these health outcomes.
keyPoints: 7 million premature deaths worldwide per year due to air pollution.
Air pollution from coal-burning power plants doubles mortality risk compared to other sources of PM2.5.
Relative risk of acute cardiovascular events increases 1–3% with short-term elevations in PM2.5; longer-term exposure amplifies this risk by ~10%.
items.reference: Health Effects of Climate Change · AIR POLLUTION AND SYNERGISTIC EFFECTS

items.subtopic: AIR POLLUTION AND SYNERGISTIC EFFECTS
items.title: Management Strategies for Climate-Sensitive Cardiovascular Disease
items.content: Clinicians should consider climate-sensitive cardiovascular disease management strategies, such as avoiding diuretic-ACE inhibitor combinations before summer or in heatwave-prone areas to prevent dehydration. Increasing potassium uptake may also be beneficial.
keyPoints: Judicious use of diuretics and ACE inhibitors is advised to avoid exacerbating dehydration during heatwaves.
Increasing potassium uptake can benefit patients with climate-sensitive cardiovascular diseases.
items.reference: Health Effects of Climate Change · AIR POLLUTION AND SYNERGISTIC EFFECTS

items.subtopic: AIR POLLUTION AND SYNERGISTIC EFFECTS
items.title: Adverse Pregnancy Outcomes Due to Air Pollution and Heat Exposure
items.content: High temperatures and air pollution are linked to adverse pregnancy outcomes such as preterm birth, low birth weight, and stillbirth. These risks are exacerbated by climate change and disproportionately affect black mothers.
keyPoints: Statistically significant association of PM2.5, ozone, and heat exposure with preterm birth, low birth weight at term, and stillbirth.
Adverse outcomes may result from systemic inflammation, oxidative stress, or direct placental injury.
Failure to reduce air pollution to WHO guideline level (10 µg/m³) compounds structural racism.
items.reference: Health Effects of Climate Change · AIR POLLUTION AND SYNERGISTIC EFFECTS

items.subtopic: HEAT-RELATED ILLNESSES | ■ NATURAL DISASTERS, COASTAL FLOODING, AND DISPLACEMENT
items.title: Chronic Kidney Disease in Agricultural Workers: Potential Mechanisms and Risk Factors
items.content: Agricultural workers in hot climates are at risk for chronic kidney disease (CKD) even without traditional risk factors. Possible mechanisms include genetic polymorphisms, nephrotoxicity from agrochemicals or heavy metals, and heat-associated injury.
keyPoints: Chronic kidney disease observed in agricultural workers in hot climates
Potential mechanisms: genetic polymorphisms, nephrotoxicity (agrochemicals/heavy metals), heat-associated injury
Risk persists even without diabetes mellitus, hypertension, glomerular disease, or HIV
items.reference: Health Effects of Climate Change · HEAT-RELATED ILLNESSES | ■ NATURAL DISASTERS, COASTAL FLOODING, AND DISPLACEMENT

items.subtopic: HEAT-RELATED ILLNESSES | ■ NATURAL DISASTERS, COASTAL FLOODING, AND DISPLACEMENT
items.title: Preventive Measures for Heat-related Illnesses in Vulnerable Populations
items.content: Heat-related illnesses like heat exhaustion and heatstroke are expected to rise with increasing temperatures. Preventive measures include developing heatwave strategies, early warning systems, mapping vulnerable populations, enhancing green spaces, and providing cool-down zones.
keyPoints: Increase in heat-related illnesses (heat cramps, heat exhaustion, heatstroke) as temperatures rise
Urban heat island effect exacerbates risk in certain communities
Preventive measures: clear heatwave strategies, early warning systems, mapping vulnerable populations, enhancing green space, cool-down zones
items.reference: Health Effects of Climate Change · HEAT-RELATED ILLNESSES | ■ NATURAL DISASTERS, COASTAL FLOODING, AND DISPLACEMENT

items.subtopic: ■ NATURAL DISASTERS, COASTAL FLOODING, AND DISPLACEMENT
items.title: Impact of Climate Change on Trauma and Mental Health
items.content: Climate change is expected to increase the intensity of storms, leading to more injuries from natural disasters. Additionally, rising temperatures are associated with increased drownings, transportation accidents, assaults, and mental health issues like anxiety, depression, PTSD, suicide, and domestic violence.
keyPoints: Higher average intensity of cyclones/hurricanes predicted
Increased trauma from intense storms
Associations between warm temperatures and increased drownings, transportation accidents, assaults
Mental health impacts: anxiety, depression, PTSD, higher rates of suicide and domestic violence
items.reference: Health Effects of Climate Change · HEAT-RELATED ILLNESSES | ■ NATURAL DISASTERS, COASTAL FLOODING, AND DISPLACEMENT

items.subtopic: FOOD SECURITY AND OCEAN RESOURCES
items.title: Impact of Climate Change on Food Security and Nutritional Quality
items.content: Climate change affects food security through decreased crop yields due to altered precipitation patterns, desertification, severe weather events, temperature extremes, GHG emissions, ocean warming, acidification, coastal inundation, and increased competition from weeds and pests. These factors lead to reduced availability of nutritious food, making it less accessible and more expensive.
keyPoints: Climate change decreases crop yields due to altered precipitation, desertification, severe weather events, temperature extremes, GHG emissions, ocean warming, acidification, coastal inundation, and increased competition from weeds and pests.
Livestock and fish production are projected to decline.
Food will become less available, less nutritious, and more expensive.
items.reference: Health Effects of Climate Change · FOOD SECURITY AND OCEAN RESOURCES

items.subtopic: FOOD SECURITY AND OCEAN RESOURCES
items.title: Benefits of Plant-Based Diets in Addressing Climate Change and Health Outcomes
items.content: Adopting plant-based diets can significantly reduce GHG emissions, improve food security in low- and middle-income countries (LMICs), and decrease mortality from major chronic diseases. By 2050, a shift to plant-based diets could reduce diet-related GHGs by 29–70% compared to current dietary practices.
keyPoints: Plant-based diets can decrease GHG emissions and improve food security in LMICs.
Potential reduction in mortality from stroke, type 2 diabetes mellitus, coronary heart disease, and cancer by 6–10%.
Diet-related GHGs could be reduced by 29–70% by 2050 compared to a reference diet.
items.reference: Health Effects of Climate Change · FOOD SECURITY AND OCEAN RESOURCES
